item.title: c. 1920s – June Dauth High School Football Photo
Louis Dauth Bakery and Confectionary
George Dauth’s Meat Market
Elsie Dauth
item.description: Elsie Dauth was born in Denver, Colorado on December 31st 1898 to Philip and Katie Dauth. She was their fifth child and the youngest of the Philip Dauth family. Her birth name was Elizabeth and she was likely named after Philip's mother Elisabeth, or Philip's late older sister Elizabeth.

item.title: c. 2020 – Andreas and Elisabetha Dauth Portrait
c. 1920s – Elsie Dauth In Black Dress
Philip Dauth
item.description: Phillip Dauth was born in Westhofen, Germany in September 1866 to Andreas and Elizabeth Dauth. In 1882, at the age of 16, he immigrated to the United States to join his siblings living in Colorado. He first made a visit to his eldest brother Louis in Fort Collins before deciding to settle in Denver near his sister Caroline (Lena) and her family.

item.title: Elizabeth Dauth
item.description: Elizabeth Dauth was born in Greeley, Colorado on June 2, 1906 to George and Florence Dauth. She was the family's second born and only daughter of the George Dauth family. She was likely named after George's mother Elisabeth, or George's late older sister Elizabeth. Elizabeth began attending Greeley High School in 1921, where she quickly became involved with a number of social clubs including the B. B. Club, K. K. Klub, and Girls Association. From 1922 to 1923 she played on the Greeley High School tennis team. She had a passion for photography and provided many of her photos for use in the Spud yearbook.

item.title: George Dauth Jr.
item.description: George Dauth Jr. was born in Greeley, Colorado on September 14, 1904 to George and Florence Dauth. He was the family's first born and nicknamed June, in reference to being named George Dauth "Junior." In 1921 June began attending Greeley High School. Here he became very active in high school sports, including basketball, football, and track. In 1922 June was selected for the All-Conference football team and played as left guard. From 1921-1924 he played for the Greeley High Wildcats, and received several awards for his abilities including earning a letter, and a gold trophy in 1924 for being captain of the Rocky Mountain Championship team.

item.title: Louis Dauth
item.description: Ludwig “Louis” Dauth was born in Westhofen, Germany on November 14, 1852 to Andrew and Elizabeth Dauth. Louis was conscripted into the North German Confederation in 1870, at the age of 18, to fight for the Prussian Army during the Franco Prussian War. He participated in “nearly every one of the hard fought battles of that short but bloody war” and was awarded two medals for his service, “one of steel, the other of brass” one of which was the Iron Cross. After the war ended he spent a year at home before immigrating to America in 1872.

item.title: Florence Yeaton
item.description: Florence May Yeaton was born in Bancraft, Nebraska on September 15th, 1878 to Zelotes and Emily Yeaton. In 1900, at the age of 21, she moved to Denver Colorado to work as a milliner. In 1901 Florence met George Dauth, who was working as a clerk at local grocery store. They married on September 8th 1903 and moved to 825 6th Street in Greeley. On September 10th, 1903, Florence announced the opening of her millinery parlor, "The Parlor Millinery" , located at 702 Ninth Avenue, Greeley. A month later Florence made plans to move her millinery to the first floor of 806 9th Street. Florence would sell her millinery parlor to Mrs. Nelson Demorest on June 2nd 1904 in anticipation of the birth of her first child, George Jr., on September 14th 1904.

item.title: Elisabeth Arnold
item.description: Elisabeth Arnold was likely born near Westhofen, Germany in the 1830s. She married Andreas Dauth who was also likely from the Westhofen area. Elisabeth and Andreas had their first child, Ludwig “Louis”, on November 14, 1852. Their first daughter Caroline “Lena” was born June 23, 1857. Elisabeth and Andreas went on to have at least five other children, Florence in 1862, Eva Katherine “Katie” on June 4th 1864, Phillip in September of 1866, George on October 24th 1868, and another daughter whose name and birth are unknown. Elisabeth spent her time at home taking care and teaching the children. Lena recalled Elisabeth teaching her how to cook for herself, before being sent to vocational school.

item.title: Andreas Dauth
item.description: Andreas Dauth was likely born near Westhofen, Germany in the 1830s. He married Elisabeth Arnold who was also likely from the Westhofen area. Andreas and Elisabeth had their first child, Ludwig “Louis”, on November 14, 1852. Their first daughter Caroline “Lena” was born June 23, 1857. Soon after, in 1858, Andreas began teaching at the local school. Andreas and Elisabeth went on to have at least five other children, Florence in 1862, Eva Katherine “Katie” on June 4th 1864, Phillip in September of 1866, George on October 24th 1868, and another daughter whose name and birth are unknown. Lena recalled being 14, in 1871, when Andrew retired from teaching, later becoming the town postmaster.

item.title: George Dauth
item.description: George Dauth was born in Westhofen, Germany on October 24, 1868 to Andrew and Elizabeth Dauth. On October 16 1884, at the age of 15, he immigrated to the United States with his older sister Katie, to join his siblings in Colorado. He first lived in Fort Collins working in his oldest brother Louis’s grocery on 223 Linden Street. While there he became a member of the International Organization of Good Templars at Collins Lodge #33. In 1888 he moved to Leadville and worked as a butcher at the E. J. Van Camp grocery store. During his time in Leadville he lived with his sister Katie, and her family. In 1894 George moved to Denver to work in Louis’s new grocery near Five Points on 2458 Washington Avenue. George spent six years in Denver transitioning from a butcher to a clerk and eventually owning his own share of Louis’s market.
